content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Over the past few years, I’ve developed a habit that probably sounds familiar if you’re someone who enjoys learning online.
Whenever I wanted to learn something new, I’d buy another course.
Python? Buy a course.
System Design? Buy a course.
AWS? Buy a course.
Machine learning? Another course.
AI agents? Yet another course.
Individually, none of those purchases felt particularly expensive. But after looking through my subscriptions recently, I realized I was paying for multiple learning platforms at the same time. Some charged monthly, others annually, and a few sold individual courses that I never quite finished. The frustrating part wasn’t even the money. It was realizing that many of those courses spent hours teaching material I already knew, just so they could eventually reach the handful of lessons I actually needed.
That changed after I started using Fenzo.ai.
I originally thought it was simply another AI learning tool. Instead, I found something that fundamentally changed how I approach learning. Rather than buying another complete course every time I wanted to learn a new topic, I could build one that matched exactly what I wanted to learn.
My Biggest Frustration With Online Courses
I don’t think online courses are the problem.
I’ve completed some fantastic courses over the years, and several of them have genuinely helped me become a better engineer. The problem is that traditional courses are designed for the average learner, not for your current knowledge level.
If you’ve already spent years writing backend code, you probably don’t need another three-hour introduction to variables, loops, or object-oriented programming before learning FastAPI. Likewise, if you understand distributed systems, you probably don’t need to sit through multiple lessons explaining what a database is before getting to the System Design topic you actually care about.
The result is that you end up paying for hundreds of lessons while only needing a small portion of the curriculum.
I Realized I Didn’t Want Another Course
After buying more subscriptions than I’d like to admit, I eventually noticed a pattern.
Every time I wanted to learn something new, I wasn’t actually looking for a complete course.
I wanted answers to very specific questions.

• How does LangGraph compare to CrewAI?

• What’s the easiest way to understand vector databases?

• How does Kafka actually work internally?

• Can someone explain consistency models using production examples instead of textbook definitions?
Traditional courses aren’t built for that. They’re linear by design.
You start at lesson one and work your way to lesson fifty, even if you only needed lesson thirty-two.
That’s incredibly efficient for beginners, but much less useful once you’ve built a reasonable technical foundation.
Then I Tried Fenzo.ai
I originally opened Fenzo.ai expecting another chatbot that summarized articles or answered programming questions.
Instead, I found something much more interesting.
Rather than searching for existing courses, I could generate an entire learning path around the exact topic I wanted to understand.
Instead of adapting my schedule to someone else’s curriculum, the curriculum adapted to me.

• If I wanted a beginner-friendly explanation of Model Context Protocol, I could generate one.

• If I wanted an advanced learning path on Agentic AI focused entirely on planning, memory, and tool orchestration, I could build that instead.

• If I wanted a crash course on Kubernetes networking without revisiting container basics, I could do that too.
That flexibility immediately stood out because it solved a problem I hadn’t realized traditional courses created.
It Felt Like Building My Own Curriculum
The feature that impressed me most wasn’t the AI itself.
It was the ability to shape the learning experience.
Instead of choosing between hundreds of existing courses, I could decide what I wanted to learn, how deep I wanted to go, and which topics I wanted to skip.
That completely changes the relationship between learner and course.
Rather than following a predefined roadmap, you’re building one that’s tailored to your own experience.
As someone who’s been working in software engineering for years, that’s surprisingly valuable because I rarely need complete beginner courses anymore. Most of the time, I just need help filling specific knowledge gaps.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It Changed How I Spend Money on Learning
This was probably the biggest surprise. I didn’t stop learning. I stopped buying as many courses.
Whenever I became interested in a new topic, my first instinct used to be searching for the highest-rated course on Udemy, Coursera, or another learning platform.
Now my first instinct is much simpler.
Can I build exactly the course I need instead? Sometimes the answer is yes.
Sometimes I’ll still buy a structured course if I’m learning an entirely new subject.
But for many intermediate and advanced topics, generating a focused learning path has been more useful than purchasing another twenty-hour course where only three hours are directly relevant.
It’s Especially Useful for Technical Topics
One thing I’ve noticed about software engineering is that learning rarely happens in a straight line.
While studying AI agents recently, I found myself jumping into vector databases, retrieval-augmented generation, embeddings, API design, prompt engineering, and evaluation frameworks, all within the same afternoon.
Traditional courses don’t handle those jumps particularly well because they’re designed around a fixed sequence.
An interactive course builder is much more flexible.
If I suddenly want to spend an hour learning only memory architectures for AI agents, I can do that.
If I want to compare Kafka with RabbitMQ before returning to System Design, I can do that too.
The learning experience follows my curiosity instead of forcing my curiosity to follow the course.
It Doesn’t Replace Great Courses
One thing I want to be clear about is that I don’t think AI-generated learning paths replace every traditional course.
There are still excellent resources I’d happily recommend.
But when you already understand the fundamentals and simply want to learn something new without committing to another expensive subscription, it’s incredibly useful.
Who I Think Will Benefit Most
After using the platform for a while, I don’t think it’s equally valuable for everyone.
If you’re learning programming for the very first time, a structured beginner course is still hard to beat because someone else has already designed an effective learning progression for you.
But if you’re a software engineer, student, AI practitioner, product manager, or lifelong learner who’s constantly exploring new technologies, an interactive course builder makes much more sense.
The more technical your work becomes, the more personalized your learning needs become as well.
That’s exactly where I found the biggest value.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

My Biggest Takeaway
The biggest lesson for me wasn’t that AI can generate courses. It was realizing how much of my learning had been shaped by whatever courses happened to exist instead of what I actually needed to learn.
That’s a subtle but important difference.
Instead of adapting myself to someone else’s curriculum, I can now start with my own curiosity and build a learning path around it.
That feels much closer to how experienced engineers actually learn throughout their careers.
Final Thoughts
Looking back, I don’t regret buying online courses. Many of them helped me build the foundation I have today, and I’d still recommend several of them to anyone starting from scratch. But as my career progressed, I realized I wasn’t looking for complete beginner-to-expert courses anymore. I was looking for focused, practical learning that matched the problems I was trying to solve right now.
That’s why Fenzo.ai has become part of my regular learning workflow. I still read books, work through documentation, and occasionally buy structured courses when I need them. The difference is that I no longer assume every new topic requires another subscription. More often than not, I can build exactly the learning path I need, explore it interactively, and move on to the next challenge without paying for dozens of lessons I’ll never watch.
For me, that has saved both time and money, and more importantly, it has made learning feel personal again.
